message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    The progression and geographical distribution of SARS coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) infection in the UK and elsewhere is unknown because typically only symptomatic individuals are diagnosed. We performed a serological study of blood donors in Scotland between the 17
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    of March and the 18
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    of May to detect neutralising antibodies to SARS-CoV-2 as a marker of past infection and epidemic progression.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Aim</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To determine if sera from blood bank donors can be used to track the emergence and progression of the SARS-CoV-2 epidemic.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    A pseudotyped SARS-CoV-2 virus microneutralisation assay was used to detect neutralising antibodies to SARS-CoV-2. The study group comprised samples from 3,500 blood donors collected in Scotland between the 17
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    of March and 19
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    of May, 2020. Controls were collected from 100 donors in Scotland during 2019.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    All samples collected on the 17
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    March, 2020 (n=500) were negative in the pseudotyped SARS-CoV-2 virus microneutralisation assay. Neutralising antibodies were detected in 6/500 donors from the 23
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    -26
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    of March. The number of samples containing neutralising antibodies did not significantly rise after the 5
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    -6
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    April until the end of the study on the 18
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    of May. We find that infections are concentrated in certain postcodes indicating that outbreaks of infection are extremely localised. In contrast, other areas remain comparatively untouched by the epidemic.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusion</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>These data indicate that sero-surveys of blood banks can serve as a useful tool for tracking the emergence and progression of an epidemic like the current SARS-CoV-2 outbreak.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Detection of neutralising antibodies to SARS coronavirus 2 to determine population exposure in Scottish blood donors between March and May 2020
